question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on this image?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image carefully for salient features. I see a dense, lobulated tuft of fine, radiating blood vessels forming a frond-like network arising from the retinal surface region; adjacent to this tuft there are more normal-appearing, larger-caliber retinal vessels.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks for the most likely diagnosis among vascular retinal/choroidal lesions. The observed lesion is a multi-branched, filamentous vascular proliferation rather than a single round saccular dilation or an isolated nodular mass.

Then I compare the image appearance to the options: the morphology is characteristic of a neovascular frond (irregular, filamentous network) consistent with retinal neovascularization as seen in proliferative diabetic retinopathy. In contrast, a retinal macroaneurysm would appear as a focal round dilation on an artery, which is not seen here. A retinal capillary hemangioma typically appears as a circumscribed lobular vascular tumor with prominent feeder and draining vessels rather than a diffuse frond. Type 1 choroidal neovascular membranes and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy tend to produce choroidal/sub-RPE vascular complexes or polyp-like round aneurysmal lesions, which do not match the surface, radiating tuft seen in this image.

Therefore, the image findings—an irregular, surface-based tuft of fine new vessels—best fit retinal neovascularization due to proliferative diabetic retinopathy.
caption: (A) FA image of an eye with PDR. Note NVE alongside the temporal inferior arcade with a sea fan configuration and leakage. (B) Corresponding OCTA image of the same complex, in which the filamentous irregular new vessels are well outlined and not obscured by fluorescein leakage. (C) OCT B-scan with flow overlay in red showing a flat complex with ILM breaching and flow signal, consistent with active disease.
sub_caption: (B) Corresponding OCTA image of the same complex, in which the filamentous irregular new vessels are well outlined and not obscured by fluorescein leakage.
image_context: Flow signal was observed in the majority of NVCs (31, 89%), namely in all the NVEs (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0001">Figures 1</xref>––<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0003">3</xref>) and in 8 (67%) of the NVDs () and in 8 (67%) of the NVDs (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0004">Figures 4</xref> and  and <xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0005">5</xref>). The en face projections showed the active NVCs as vascular loops or filamentous irregular new vessels (). The en face projections showed the active NVCs as vascular loops or filamentous irregular new vessels (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0001">Figures 1</xref>––<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0005">5</xref>). EVP, observed in OCTA as an intense growth of irregular small-caliber vessels, was found in 13 (37%) NVCs (9 (39%) of NVEs and 4 (33%) of NVDs). In the subgroup of NVEs, 20 (87%) had adjacent NPAs, identified as retinal areas of absent flow signal or sparse capillary density (). EVP, observed in OCTA as an intense growth of irregular small-caliber vessels, was found in 13 (37%) NVCs (9 (39%) of NVEs and 4 (33%) of NVDs). In the subgroup of NVEs, 20 (87%) had adjacent NPAs, identified as retinal areas of absent flow signal or sparse capillary density (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0002">Figures 2</xref> and  and <xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0003">3</xref>). IRMAs were noted next to 16 (70%) of the NVEs (). IRMAs were noted next to 16 (70%) of the NVEs (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0002">Figures 2</xref> and  and <xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0003">3</xref>). There was no relation between NPAs and IRMAs adjacent to the NVE (p=0.21). With respect to the morphologic classification, 13 (57%) were type 1 (). There was no relation between NPAs and IRMAs adjacent to the NVE (p=0.21). With respect to the morphologic classification, 13 (57%) were type 1 (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0002">Figure 2</xref>), 0 (0%) were type 2, 5 (22%) were type 3 (), 0 (0%) were type 2, 5 (22%) were type 3 (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0001">Figure 1</xref>) and 5 (22%) were classified as other. No association was found between the morphologic classification and NVE type or posterior hyaloid state (respectively, p=0.65 and p=0.72). In the subgroup of NVDs, OCTA showed flow signal within 8 (67%) of the NVCs, indicating disease activity () and 5 (22%) were classified as other. No association was found between the morphologic classification and NVE type or posterior hyaloid state (respectively, p=0.65 and p=0.72). In the subgroup of NVDs, OCTA showed flow signal within 8 (67%) of the NVCs, indicating disease activity (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0004">Figures 4</xref> and  and <xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0005">5</xref>), as corroborated with complementary testing. The remaining 4 (33%) did not show intrinsic flow signal within the complexes and were not associated with EVP and were labeled inactive NVDs (), as corroborated with complementary testing. The remaining 4 (33%) did not show intrinsic flow signal within the complexes and were not associated with EVP and were labeled inactive NVDs (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0006">Figure 6</xref>). Posterior shadowing (). Posterior shadowing (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="OPTH-14-3351-g0006">Figure 6</xref>) was identified in 6 (50%) NVDs, 4 (67%) of them inactive, although the association was not significant (p=0.06).) was identified in 6 (50%) NVDs, 4 (67%) of them inactive, although the association was not significant (p=0.06).Figure 1(A) FA image of an eye with PDR. Note NVE alongside the temporal inferior arcade with a sea fan configuration and leakage. (B) Corresponding OCTA image of the same complex, in which the filamentous irregular new vessels are well outlined and not obscured by fluorescein leakage. (C) OCT B-scan with flow overlay in red showing a flat complex with ILM breaching and flow signal, consistent with active disease.Abbreviations: FA, fluorescein angiography; ILM, internal limiting membrane; NVE, neovascularization elsewhere; OCT, optical coherence tomography; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography; PDR, proliferative diabetic retinopathy.Figure 2Montage showing two NVEs. (A) Late FA image shows leakage associated with both NVEs. (B, top) In OCTA both NVEs appear as vascular loops with irregular new vessels, each one circled by a dashed ellipse. (B, bottom) Areas with absent flow signal adjacent to NVEs (✢) are seen, corresponding to the capillary nonperfusion evidenced on FA. (C and D) B-scans of the NVEs show ILM breaching and flow signal, indicating disease activity, in agreement with the FA. Each NVE is circled by a colored dashed ellipse matching the en face image in (B, top).Abbreviations: FA, fluorescein angiography; ILM, internal limiting membrane; NVE, neovascularization elsewhere; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography; PDR, proliferative diabetic retinopathy.Figure 3(A) En face OCTA 8 x 8 mm montage showing significant areas of absent flow signal (✢) and a temporal NVE. (B) Magnification of the area within the dotted rectangle in (A) shows the detailed neovascular complex with associated retinal nonperfusion areas (✢) and flow signal in the B-scan (C).Abbreviations: NVE, neovascularization elsewhere; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography.Figure 4Imaging study of a neovascular complex of the right eye. (A) The OCTA image clearly shows the new vessels, seen as an irregular vascular loop on a healthy retinal vessel (encircled by the dashed line). (B) Note that on the FA image, no clear leakage is identified in the corresponding area depicted by the dashed rectangle. (C) The matching OCT B-scan with flow overlay clearly shows flow signal and the new vessel breaching the ILM (encircled by the dashed ellipse).Abbreviations: FA, fluorescein angiography; ILM, internal limiting membrane; OCT, optical coherence tomography; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography.Figure 5Top row (A and B): OCTA shows the morphology of 2 cases of NVD – new vessels with terminal loops and irregular proliferation of small-caliber vessels. (A) was classified as having EVP. Bottom row (C and D): Co-registered OCT B-scan images with flow overlay in red showing layer segmentation of the corresponding en face OCT angiograms and flow signal within the NVDs, indicating disease activity.Abbreviations: EVP, exuberant vascular proliferation; NVD, neovascularization of the disc; OCT, optical coherence tomography; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography.Figure 6Example of treated inactive NVD. (A) OCTA en face projections of the optic disc reveals posterior shadowing which is correlated with fibrotic tissue and there is no EVP. (B) Color fundus photograph demonstrates a whitish fibrotic membrane (arrowheads) consistent with quiescent NVD. (C) Matching OCT B-scan with flow overlay shows protrusion from the disc and no flow signal within the complex, in agreement with disease inactivity.Abbreviations: EVP, exuberant vascular proliferation; NVD, neovascularization of the disc; OCT, optical coherence tomography; OCTA, optical coherence tomography angiography.
